skill.name: gif-sticker-maker
skill.description: Create four captioned animated GIF stickers from a user photo, logo, object, or pet. The workflow uses MiniMax image and video generation, then converts MP4 outputs to GIF files with ffmpeg.
skill.summary: Create four captioned animated GIF stickers from photos with MiniMax and ffmpeg.
skill.author: MiniMax-AI

security_audit.summary: Static findings for network access, credential use, local file writes, and ffmpeg subprocess execution are confirmed, but they match the documented sticker-generation workflow. No evidence of malicious intent or prompt injection was found; publication should include a warning about external API upload of user media and local command execution.

medium_findings.title: External API Upload of User Media and Prompts
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The image and video scripts send prompts, subject references, and generated asset requests to MiniMax API endpoints. This is expected for the skill, but it exposes user photos and captions to an external service.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network calls are direct and semantically tied to generation and download. The risk is privacy exposure, not confirmed exfiltration beyond the declared service.

medium_findings.title: Bearer Token Sent to Configurable API Base
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The scripts read MINIMAX_API_KEY and MINIMAX_API_BASE from the environment and attach the bearer token to API requests. A malicious or mistaken API base value could receive the credential.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The credential and API base usage are clear in the code. This is a legitimate configuration pattern, but the absence of endpoint allowlisting keeps the risk material.

medium_findings.title: Local File Read and Write Operations on User Paths
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The scripts read subject images, write downloaded media, create output directories, and delete temporary palette files. This is required for the workflow, but user-supplied paths can affect local files.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: File operations are explicit and expected for media processing. The main concern is unrestricted output path use, not evidence of hidden file harvesting.

low_findings.title: ffmpeg Subprocess Execution Uses Argument Lists
low_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The converter runs ffmpeg on user-provided MP4 files. Command injection risk is reduced because shell execution is not used, but media parser exposure remains.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess calls are real, but arguments are passed as lists with no shell=True. The residual risk depends on ffmpeg and untrusted media inputs.

low_findings.title: Operational Identifiers Printed to Console
low_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The video script prints task IDs, file IDs, prompt previews, image paths, and part of the download URL. This is useful debugging output but may expose sensitive workflow details in shared logs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The printed values are visible in the code and may be sensitive in some environments. There is no evidence they are transmitted elsewhere.

low_findings.title: Static Alerts for Weak Crypto, Obfuscation, and Markdown Backticks Dismissed
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The weak cryptography alerts point to argparse descriptions or markdown content, and base64 is used to encode or decode image bytes. The SKILL.md backtick findings are documentation examples, not Ruby execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line review shows no weak cryptographic primitive or executable Ruby backtick pattern. Base64 handles image transport and is not obfuscation in this context.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Command Invocation
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill invokes ffmpeg through subprocess.run to convert MP4 files to GIF files. This should be run only with trusted media inputs and an up-to-date ffmpeg installation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command execution pattern is present and intentional. The code avoids shell execution, which lowers confidence in command injection specifically.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network Requests with Bearer Authentication
dangerous_patterns.description: The scripts make POST and GET requests using a bearer token from MINIMAX_API_KEY. This is necessary for MiniMax generation, but credentials must be scoped and protected.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The authorization header and HTTP calls are explicit. The intended MiniMax use is legitimate, but credential-bearing network access is still a significant pattern.

dangerous_patterns.title: User Media Encoded for Remote Generation
dangerous_patterns.description: Subject reference and first-frame images are read from local paths and base64-encoded for API submission. Users should not process sensitive photos unless they accept MiniMax handling.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The image encoding flow is clear and linked to remote API payloads. This confirms external handling of local media without indicating hidden collection.

content.value_statement: Making matched sticker GIFs from a photo normally requires image generation, animation, and conversion steps. This skill guides Claude, Codex, or Claude Code through a MiniMax workflow that creates four captioned animated stickers.
actual_capabilities: Creates four sticker concepts for waving, laughing, crying, and heart gestures.
Generates static 3D cartoon sticker images with bottom captions.
Uses person reference photos to preserve facial likeness when supported by MiniMax.
Animates generated sticker images into short MP4 videos.
Converts MP4 outputs into GIF files with ffmpeg.
Supports default captions in several languages and short custom captions.
limitations: Requires MINIMAX_API_KEY, MINIMAX_API_BASE, Python dependencies, and ffmpeg.
Uploads prompts and selected user images to the configured MiniMax API endpoint.
Subject reference mode is intended for people and may not work for pets, objects, or logos.
Output quality depends on MiniMax model behavior and caption stability during animation.

use_cases.title: Personal Sticker Set
use_cases.description: Turn a selfie into four expressive GIF stickers for chat apps or social posts.

use_cases.title: Pet Reaction Pack
use_cases.description: Create a small animated sticker pack from a pet photo with short reaction captions.

use_cases.title: Brand Mascot Animation
use_cases.description: Transform a logo or mascot image into playful animated stickers for campaign assets.

prompt_templates.title: Create Default Stickers
prompt_templates.prompt: Create four animated GIF stickers from this photo using the default captions for my language.

prompt_templates.title: Use Custom Captions
prompt_templates.scenario: User provides exact text for each sticker.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create four GIF stickers from this image with these captions: Hi there, So funny, Miss you, Love this.

prompt_templates.title: Make a Pet Pack
prompt_templates.scenario: Pet-focused generation without person reference mode.
prompt_templates.prompt: Make a cute animated sticker pack from this pet photo. Use short captions and keep the pet markings recognizable.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare Brand Stickers
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request for a logo or mascot workflow.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a four-part GIF sticker set from this logo. Preserve the brand colors, use clean captions, and deliver final GIF files.

output_examples.input: A selfie with default English captions.
output_examples.output: Four GIF stickers are delivered: a waving sticker, a laughing sticker, a crying sticker, and a heart gesture sticker.

output_examples.input: A pet photo with custom captions.
output_examples.output: The result is a matched animated sticker set with short captions and consistent white-background styling.

output_examples.input: A logo image for a campaign mascot.
output_examples.output: The output is a small branded GIF pack that keeps the logo colors and turns the mark into a toy-like character.

best_practices: Use clear source photos with one main subject and enough visible detail.
Keep captions to one to three words so they remain readable in GIFs.
Review the configured MiniMax API base before sending private or sensitive images.
anti_patterns: Do not use crowded group photos when a single sticker subject is required.
Do not use long captions, small text, or mixed languages in one sticker pack.
Do not process confidential images unless external MiniMax handling is approved.

faq.question: Does this skill create the GIF files directly?
faq.answer: Yes. It generates images, animates them as MP4 files, then converts the videos to GIF files with ffmpeg.

faq.question: Does it need a MiniMax API key?
faq.answer: Yes. The scripts require MINIMAX_API_KEY and MINIMAX_API_BASE before generation can start.

faq.question: Can I use custom captions?
faq.answer: Yes. Short captions work best, and the workflow asks whether to use custom captions or defaults.

faq.question: Will it preserve a person's likeness?
faq.answer: It can pass a person photo as a subject reference when the MiniMax API supports that mode.

faq.question: Can it make stickers from pets or logos?
faq.answer: Yes. Pets, objects, and logos are supported through prompt description, but subject reference mode is for people.

faq.question: What security warning should users know?
faq.answer: The workflow sends selected media and prompts to the configured MiniMax API endpoint and runs ffmpeg locally.
